name: Brainstorm: Can a machine be creative?

utterances.speaker: Justin
utterances.text: Okay so the new image models — some of that stuff is genuinely beautiful. I think we have to call it creative.

utterances.speaker: Filip
utterances.text: I disagree — it's pattern-matching on millions of human paintings, that's mimicry not creation.

utterances.speaker: Justin
utterances.text: Hmm, but humans also learn by copying first. Every art student starts there.

utterances.speaker: Filip
utterances.text: Fair, but they eventually want to say something. The model doesn't want anything.

utterances.speaker: Justin
utterances.text: Okay yeah, intent. That's the real gap.

utterances.speaker: Justin
utterances.text: Wait though — does the audience care about intent? People love the outputs.

utterances.speaker: Filip
utterances.text: That's not the right test. People also love sunsets and we don't call those creative.

utterances.speaker: Justin
utterances.text: Practical question though — for graphic designers using these tools, does the philosophy even matter?

utterances.speaker: Filip
utterances.text: For their job, no. For the field long-term, yes.

utterances.speaker: Justin
utterances.text: Hmm. I'd push back a bit — the field has survived photography, photoshop, all of it.

utterances.speaker: Justin
utterances.text: So circling back — tool, not creator? At least for now?

utterances.speaker: Filip
utterances.text: Yeah, I can live with that — powerful tool, no inner life. We'll fight about it again in five years.
